seoFaqs.question: What is the Ugra Narasimha Stotram and which form does it invoke?
seoFaqs.answer: The Ugra Narasimha Stotram (उग्र नरसिंह स्तोत्रम्) is a powerful eight-verse Sanskrit hymn dedicated specifically to Ugra Narasimha — the fierce, wrathful form of Lord Narasimha who emerged from the temple-pillar at the twilight hour to slay Hiranyakashipu and protect Prahlada. Unlike the gentler Lakshmi-Narasimha or Yoga-Narasimha worship-forms, Ugra Narasimha is the supreme bhaya-haran (fear-removing) and shatru-nashaka (enemy-destroying) form, invoked in cases of grave danger, persecution by powerful enemies, supernatural threats and severe afflictions. The stotra opens with the celebrated Ugram Viram Maha-Vishnum mantra, describes Narasimha's fierce iconography (jvala-mala-akulam, karala-damshtra, nakhayudham, jvala-kesham, trilochanam), narrates his emergence from the pillar, and ends with the heart-cry trahi mam trahi mam — protect me, protect me. (Sources: Narasimha Purana; classical Vaishnava ugra-stotra tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the difference between Ugra Narasimha and Lakshmi Narasimha?
seoFaqs.answer: Ugra Narasimha and Lakshmi Narasimha are two principal worship-forms of the same Vishnu avatara, distinguished by mood, iconography and worship-purpose. Ugra Narasimha (also called Vidaaraka or Sthauna Narasimha) is the fierce form depicted at the moment of Hiranyakashipu's slaying — lion-faced, fang-bared, blood-stained, intestine-garlanded, eyes blazing, mane flaming — worshipped for the destruction of grave enemies, removal of severe afflictions, breaking of black-magic, and protection from supernatural threats. Lakshmi Narasimha (also Shanta or Yoga Narasimha) is the peaceful post-slaying form with Lakshmi seated on his lap, worshipped for prosperity, marital harmony, family welfare, and gentle daily protection — the typical grihastha (householder) form. Ugra worship traditionally requires stricter ritual purity and is often undertaken under guru-guidance; Lakshmi-Narasimha is the safer householder form. (Sources: Pancharatra Agama; Vaikhanasa Agama; classical Vaishnava theology.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the significance of Narasimha emerging at sandhya from the pillar?
seoFaqs.answer: The Ugra Narasimha Stotram verse 8 — Stambhodbhavam Maha-sattvam Sandhya-kala-samudbhavam — captures two of the most theologically significant features of Narasimha's avatara, both of which were necessary to circumvent Brahma's boon to Hiranyakashipu. (1) Stambhodbhavam — emerging from the temple-pillar — confirms Vishnu's omnipresence (sarvatra-sthitatva) since the asura had asked 'Is your Vishnu in this pillar?' Prahlada's reply 'Yes, my Lord is everywhere' was vindicated as the pillar burst open. (2) Sandhya-kala-samudbhavam — appearing at the twilight hour — circumvents the boon clause that he could not be killed 'by day or by night', since sandhya is technically neither. This precise time of appearance is observed at every Narasimha Jayanti by performing the abhishekam at sunset rather than morning. The pillar-emergence and sandhya-appearance together demonstrate the precision with which Vishnu's grace operates within and beyond the bounds of any boon. (Sources: Bhagavata Purana 7.8; Narasimha Purana.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should the Ugra Narasimha Stotram be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Ugra Narasimha Stotram is most powerful when recited at sandhya (twilight) — both morning and especially evening, replicating the time of Narasimha's avatara. It is especially auspicious on Saturdays (Shanivar — when Ugra Narasimha is invoked to pacify severe Saturn afflictions, particularly during sade-sati and dhaiyya), on Swati nakshatra (Narasimha's birth nakshatra), on every Chaturdashi tithi (especially Krishna Chaturdashi), and supremely on Narasimha Jayanti (Vaishakha Shukla Chaturdashi at sunset). For acute distress, devotees recite the stotra 11, 21 or 108 times facing east at twilight, before a Narasimha murti or yantra, with a ghee lamp and til-oil lamp lit. Owing to the ugra (fierce) nature of this form, traditional acharyas recommend that serious Ugra-Narasimha sadhana be undertaken only with proper initiation and ritual purity. (Sources: classical Narasimha sadhana paddhati; tantric ugra-deva worship norms.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Ugra Narasimha Stotram bestow according to its phalashruti?
seoFaqs.answer: The closing phalashruti of the Ugra Narasimha Stotram declares: ugra-Nrisimha-stotram yah pathed bhakti-samyutah, sarva-bhiti-vinasham cha sarva-shatru-kshayam labhet — 'Whoever recites this Ugra-Narasimha stotram with devotion obtains the destruction of all fears (sarva-bhiti-vinasham) and the destruction of all enemies (sarva-shatru-kshaya).' Verse 6 additionally promises sarva-roga-prashamanam (pacification of all diseases) and sarva-bhuta-bhayapaham (removal of fear from all beings — including bhuta-preta, supernatural entities). Traditional commentaries extend the benefits to: protection from the most severe asuric attacks; breaking of abhicharika prayoga (black-magic); relief from grave Shani afflictions; healing of mysterious or undiagnosable illnesses; victory in seemingly hopeless legal and worldly conflicts; and the destruction of inner kleshas through Narasimha's fierce grace. The stotra is considered the supreme protective stotra for situations of grave danger. (Sources: Ugra Narasimha phalashruti; Narasimha Purana.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the Trahi Mam Trahi Mam cry in this stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The fifth verse of the Ugra Narasimha Stotram contains one of the most heart-rending bhakti pleas in Sanskrit literature: Trahi mam trahi mam Natha Ugra-Nrisimha Keshava, mama shatrun vinashaya mam raksha sarvadapadbhyah — 'Protect me, protect me, O Lord, Ugra Narasimha, Keshava! Destroy my enemies; protect me from all calamities.' The doubled trahi mam ('save me, save me') captures the desperation of the devotee in extreme distress, paralleling Draupadi's desperate cry to Krishna in the Mahabharata, Gajendra's cry to Vishnu in the Gajendra Moksha, and Prahlada's own desperate flight from Hiranyakashipu's torments. This verse is traditionally recited as a stand-alone mantra at moments of acute danger — recited 11 or 21 times by devotees facing immediate physical, emotional or supernatural threat — invoking the immediate appearance of Narasimha as he appeared for Prahlada. (Sources: Ugra Narasimha Stotram verse 5; classical bhakti rakshana tradition.)
